title: Dog Brown Waxy Buildup in Ears: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Noticed brown wax in your dog's ears? Learn about common causes like yeast or mites and why this condition is generally a low-urgency concern.
content_html:
TL;DR: Brown waxy buildup in a dog’s ear is often normal earwax, but excessive, smelly, or gritty discharge may indicate a yeast infection or ear mites. While usually low urgency, seek veterinary care if your dog shows signs of discomfort, redness, or loss of balance.

What is the brown waxy buildup in my dog's ears?

Brown discharge or waxy buildup in a dog's ears is typically a combination of natural earwax (cerumen), skin oils, and environmental debris. However, if the buildup is excessive, it often indicates an underlying issue such as a yeast infection or ear mites. Yeast infections frequently produce a dark, moist, musty-smelling debris, while ear mite infestations often resemble dry, dark coffee grounds.

Is brown earwax in my dog a veterinary emergency?

In most cases, brown earwax is considered a Low Urgency level and is rarely an immediate medical emergency. However, you should schedule a veterinary appointment if the buildup is accompanied by the following symptoms:

  • Persistent scratching or head shaking
  • A foul odor or visible redness
  • Significant head tilting
  • Appearing off-balance or disoriented (requires prompt attention to rule out a middle-ear infection)

How does taking a photo of my dog's ear help with triage?

Capturing a clear, well-lit photo of the inside of your dog's ear flap and the entrance to the canal can be incredibly helpful for triage because:

  • It allows a professional to assess the texture, color, and volume of the discharge.
  • It helps differentiate between normal wax accumulation and signs of an active infection.
  • It ensures your pet receives the correct advice and priority level before you even step into the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs presenting with brown waxy buildup in the ears, suggestive of otitis externa, the key to treatment involves identifying the underlying cause and determining if the condition is curable. Systemic antimicrobial therapy is crucial in cases with neutrophils or rod-type bacteria on cytology, therapeutic failure with topical antimicrobials, chronic recurring ear infections, and all cases of suspected otitis media, which is a common cause of recurrent otitis externa. Yeast infections can be addressed with oral ketoconazole (5 mg/kg/day for 15-30 days), while itraconazole (2-3 mg/kg/day for 15-30 days or one week on/one week off) is preferred in cats. Irritating substances like home remedies, vinegar dilutions, and powders should be avoided as they can exacerbate inflammation and opportunistic infections; thin films are preferred over ointments for topical medication. In cases of chronic, obstructive, unilateral otitis externa, biopsies should be considered to rule out neoplastic changes. Radiography of the osseous bullae is indicated when visualization of the tympanic membrane is inadequate, otitis media is suspected, or neurologic signs are present; however, CT or MRI should be performed for severe, chronic cases.

Chapter: Dermatology, Neurology, Diagnostic Imaging

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 529)

faqs.question: Can I use Q-tips to clean out the brown wax?
faqs.answer: It is best to avoid cotton swabs (Q-tips) as they can push debris deeper into the ear canal and risk rupturing the eardrum. Use a vet-approved liquid cleaner and cotton balls instead.

faqs.question: Does brown wax always mean my dog has an infection?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Some dogs naturally produce more wax than others. However, if the wax is accompanied by a bad smell, redness, or signs of pain, an infection is likely.

faqs.question: How often should I check my dog's ears?
faqs.answer: Checking your dog's ears once a week is a good habit. This allows you to catch changes in color or odor early before they become painful infections.
